file_name: Reviewer Criteria for New Data Preregistrations.pdf

pages_preview.text_preview: New Data Preregistration Reviewer Criteria The lists below are intended to guide reviewers in evaluating New Data preregistrations for SCORE. You don’t need to fill out a copy of the checklist, but should use it as a guide to the types of information that should be present in a finished preregistration - remember that not all items will apply to all projects. For each question, the response should include a complete description (a statement of the research plan in enough detail for another group to faithfully implement), list of deviations (clearly stating what has added, omitted, or changed from the original study), and rationale (altogether, is there a strong justification of why decisions are consistent with a good-faith replication of the original claim). At the end of the list are the final questions you should evaluate when deciding whether a preregistration is complete. Study Information 1. Title (No evaluation is necessary.) 2. Authors and Affiliations (No evaluation is necessary.) 3. Description of study For Replication projects, they should not depart from the analysis of the original study, and an important part of the review process will be to make sure the lab does not drift away from this description. For Generalizability projects, please check and ensure that the differences between the original study and the proposed project remain consistent and understandable.

Design Plan 4. Hypotheses Every preregistration has a single hypothesis called H*, which is the specific test used to evaluate the SCORE focal claim. In addition, labs may choose to add additional hypotheses that are closely related to H*, either from the original study or new to this project (i.e. to give an alternative analysis of the focal claim). Any additional hypotheses should be extremely related to H*. If additional hypotheses seem more like extensions or less related to H*, these should be put into their own, separate pre-registration. Review Criteria: ● Description: Does the list above provide enough detail to evaluate what confirmatory hypotheses this preregistration is designed to test? ○ Are the hypotheses labeled H1, H2, etc., with the SCORE focal hypothesis as H*? ○ Are the listed hypotheses specific, concise, clearly testable, and specified at the level of operationalized variables? ○ Are hypotheses identified as directional or non-directional, and, if applicable, have the direction of hypotheses been stated? (Example: “Customers’ mean choice satisfaction will be higher in the CvSS architecture condition than in the standard attribute-by-attribute architecture condition.” ○ Replications: Is the H* listed here equivalent to the statement in #3? ○ Generalizability: Does the H* listed here match the generalization being made in the study? ● Deviations: Does the list of hypotheses/tests indicate whether additional hypotheses are taken from the original study or modified/added by the team? ● Rationale: Does the description provide a rationale for the inclusion of any additional hypotheses (i.e. that they are logical deductions/operationalizations of the selected SCORE claim). 5. Study Type (No evaluation is necessary)

6. Blinding (multiple choice) Review Criteria: Check that the selections on this question make sense with the description given in Question 7 7. Blinding (free response) Review Criteria: ● Description: Does the preregistration provide sufficient detail about whether/how blinding will be conducted? This might include: ○ Does the preregistration comment on whether participants will be blinded to the study’s hypotheses? How will this be accomplished? ○ Does the preregistration comment on whether participants will be blinded to the treatment conditions? How will this be accomplished? ○ Does the preregistration comment on whether personnel will be blinded to the study’s hypotheses? How will this be accomplished? ○ Does the preregistration comment on whether personnel will be blinded to the study’s treatment conditions? How will this be accomplished? ● Deviations: Does the preregistration provide sufficient detail about how study blinding deviates from or is congruent with the original study? ○ If the original materials do not provide substantial detail on the blinding procedures, is it clear what additions the replication is making? ● Rationale: Did the preregistration provide a justification for why decisions regarding blinding procedures are consistent with a good-faith replication effort of the focal claim? 8. Study Design Depending on the type of study, the answer to this question may be short or very lengthy. Many topics (such as the target population and how members of that population are identified or recruited) may be addressed either here and/or in another part of the preregistration - the

replicating/reproducing team has flexibility to distribute this information across questions in the way that makes most sense for the project. Review Criteria: ● Description: Does the preregistration provide sufficient detail about the study design? This might include: ○ Does the preregistration specify the unit of analysis? ○ Does the preregistration describe how many treatment conditions will be used in the study, and how many conditions participants will be exposed to? ○ Does the preregistration include a description of the target population and study setting along those dimensions that are salient to the focal claim (women 18 and over interviewed while at a shopping mall located in a Latin American country)? ○ Does it describe any counterbalancing that is required? ○ Does the preregistration include a description of the empirical strategy? For many experimental studies, this section may be extremely succinct. For observational studies or experimental studies that focus on the impacts of post-randomization (i.e. not manipulated) outcomes, inferences involving assumptions about causal effects should provide concise references to these assumptions - either in equation or text form. ● Deviations: Does the preregistration provide sufficient detail about how the study design deviates from or is congruent with the study design employed in the original study? ○ Were the similarities/differences in the target populations and study settings between the original and replication study described? ○ Were similarities/differences in the experimental design or empirical strategy between the original and replication study described? ○ If materials from the original study do not provide details on the study design, is it clear what additions the replication is making? ● Rationale: Did the preregistration provide a justification for why decisions regarding the study design are consistent with a good-faith replication effort of the focal claim? 9. Randomization Review Criteria: ● Description: Does the preregistration provide sufficient detail about randomization procedures?

○ Does the preregistration describe the level at which randomization takes place? (Examples include randomization within subject, blocked by condition, by cluster, etc.) ○ Does the preregistration describe the method of randomization that is used? (Examples include simple, block, stratified, and adaptive covariate randomization, etc.) ○ Does the preregistration describe how the randomization is implemented? (Examples include Kish grid, random number table, statistical software package, etc.) ○ Does the preregistration draw a clear distinction regarding which factors involved in the test(s) of the focal claim are randomized or manipulated and which factors are not. ● Deviations: Does the preregistration provide sufficient detail about how the randomization procedures deviates from or are congruent with the study design employed in the original study? ○ If the original materials do not provide substantial detail on randomization, is it clear what additions the replication is making? ● Rationale: Did the preregistration provide a justification for why decisions regarding randomization are consistent with a good-faith replication effort of the focal claim? Sampling Plan 10. Existing data (multiple choice) (No evaluation necessary - this categorization is made by the SCORE research scientist) 11. Explanation of existing data For projects using the New Data Preregistration form, this should not need evaluation, as preregistration is happening prior to data collection. For projects using the Existing Data Preregistration form, please evaluate that this section gives details related to the response in the previous section, Section 10.

12. Data collection procedures Note to Reviewers: Please make sure to review the materials that the labs have uploaded, in addition to their description above. Remember that not all studies will need to supply every type of information, but you should check that (for instance), if the protocol involves a special piece of equipment, the equipment is described. A link to these materials is available on the cover sheet of the preregistration draft. Review Criteria: ● Description: Does the preregistration describe the target population, and how members of the target population are sampled for the study? ○ Does the description include information about recruitment of participants, including context, location, and compensation offered? If data will be collected with the assistance of a firm (e.g. Qualtrics), is the firm identified? ○ If participants are embedded in an institution (e.g. nurses working in a hospital), does the preregistration comment on who will be contacted at the institution to facilitate the recruitment, and how will participant anonymity be maintained? ○ Does the preregistration comment on how participants will be compensated for their participation? ○ Does the preregistration comment on what information the study participants will provided during recruitment and consenting? ○ Does the preregistration describe how they will track the percentage of participants approached who agree to participate? ○ Does the description include demographic or other inclusion/exclusion criteria that are either used to target recruitment or are administered to participants during a study (e.g. ‘Participants are asked to report their age, because participants under 20 years will not be included in the analytic sample’.) ○ Does the protocol include information about how participants give consent to participate, including whether or not they give consent for their data to be shared and/or are informed about sharing of deidentified data? ○ Does the protocol provide links to materials that will be presented to participants, including variation or structure in those materials relevant to the experimental design? These should be stored in the OSF project associated with this project. ○ Does the description make clear how participants are assigned to experimental conditions, or how within-participant condition manipulations are assigned? ○ Does the preregistration include the specification of any special equipment necessary for data collection? ○ Does the preregistration discuss how many observations or measurements per unit will be included in the focal analysis?
